claim: Available public data sources are sufficient for country-level AMR burden estimation and global surveillance-gap framing (Murray et al. 2022, WHO GLASS, WHO AMR fact sheet), but no public dataset provides sub-national AMR resistance rates or laboratory-capacity data at the granularity needed to identify which districts within LMICs have the highest AMR burden relative to surveillance capacity. IHME GBD Compare provides modeled country-level estimates with wide uncertainty. ResistanceMap provides sparse country-level resistance rates with inconsistent LMIC coverage. Global-PPS provides hospital-level prescribing data but not resistance rates. The sub-national surveillance gap cannot be mapped with currently available public data alone.
failure_modes: A reader treats Murray et al. modeled burden estimates as direct surveillance measurements for specific countries.
A reader treats GLASS enrollment as equivalent to functional surveillance capacity, when enrollment does not guarantee data quality, completeness, or sub-national coverage.
A reader uses ResistanceMap country-level rates to draw sub-national conclusions about surveillance gaps.
A reader treats Global-PPS prescribing data as resistance surveillance data.
A reader uses GBD Compare uncertainty intervals as evidence of surveillance quality, when they reflect model assumptions, not measurement density.
A policy maker uses the surveillance-gap framing to justify restricting antibiotic access rather than investing in laboratory capacity.
kill_condition: A reviewer identifies a public dataset providing sub-national AMR resistance rates or laboratory-capacity data at district or facility level for at least 10 LMICs with consistent methodology and less than 2-year reporting lag, or finds that GLASS sub-national reporting has expanded to cover districts in at least 20 LMICs with quality-verified data.
limitations: The claim is about public data availability for sub-national surveillance-gap analysis, not about actual AMR rates or surveillance quality in specific countries.
The assessment of 'no public dataset provides sub-national data' is an inference from reviewing available data portals, not a systematic survey of all national AMR surveillance databases.
Some LMICs may have national surveillance systems that are not publicly accessible or not in English; these were not reviewed.

GLASS reports (separate from the program page) may contain more granular data than the program page suggests; this claim is based on the program page and fact sheet, not a full review of every GLASS report.
